description: Add a 30-second first-drag popup for the pub-sub component. Content: WHAT: A messaging pattern where publishers send messages to a topic and all subscribers to that topic receive a copy — enabling one-to-many broadcast without the publisher knowing who the subscribers are. USE WHEN: One event needs to trigger multiple independent actions: a new order might need to notify inventory, shipping, analytics, and email services simultaneously. COMMON MISTAKE: Students confuse pub-sub with a queue — in a queue, each message is processed by ONE consumer; in pub-sub, each message is delivered to ALL subscribers. Example: Google Pub/Sub, AWS SNS, Apache Kafka (in topic mode) — Google uses Pub/Sub to fan out billions of events across internal services.

title: Comparison Challenge: Cache-aside vs Write-through for consistency
description: Comparison challenge prompt: 'Your system shows stale data after updates. Compare cache-aside and write-through caching patterns. Which gives better consistency and what are the tradeoffs?' Expected answer: Cache-aside (lazy loading): App checks cache first; on miss, reads from DB and populates cache. On write, app updates DB and invalidates (deletes) cache. Stale data risk: Between DB write and cache invalidation, a concurrent read can re-populate the cache with old data (race condition). Write-through: App writes to cache and DB simultaneously (or cache writes to DB). Cache is always consistent. Tradeoff: Write latency increases (must write both), and you cache data that may never be read (wasted memory). Best practices: (1) Cache-aside with short TTL (5 min) limits staleness window. (2) Write-through for data that's frequently read after write (user profiles). (3) For strict consistency, use read-your-own-writes: after a write, read from primary DB instead of cache for that user's next request. Learning objective: No caching pattern gives perfect consistency for free — each has a staleness window or performance cost.

title: Comparison Challenge: Active-Active vs Active-Passive failover

Expected answer: Active-passive: One primary handles all traffic; a standby waits idle. On failure, traffic switches to standby (30s-5min failover). Achieves ~99.9% (8.7 hours/year). Problem: The standby is wasted capacity, and failover time may exceed your SLA budget. Active-active: Both data centers handle traffic simultaneously. For 99.99%, active-active is required — active-passive failover time alone can consume most of your annual downtime budget. Learning objective: Higher availability SLAs require active-active, which introduces data consistency challenges that must be explicitly designed for.

title: Bug-Finding Challenge: Payment system with no idempotency on retry
description: Bug-finding challenge prompt: 'Here is a payment flow: (1) User clicks Pay. (2) Server calls Stripe. (3) On timeout, server retries the Stripe call. (4) On success, server marks order as paid. What is the critical bug?' Expected answer: The bug is NO IDEMPOTENCY KEY on the Stripe call. When the first call times out, it may have succeeded (Stripe charged the card, but the response was lost). The retry sends a second charge request, and without an idempotency key, Stripe processes it as a NEW charge — the user is double-charged. Fix: (1) Generate a unique idempotency key (order_id or UUID) before the first attempt. (2) Send the same key on every retry. (3) Stripe deduplicates: if it already processed a request with that key, it returns the original response without charging again. (4) Store the payment state as 'pending' before calling Stripe, and update to 'completed' or 'failed' based on the response. Learning objective: In distributed systems, 'retry on failure' is only safe with idempotency. Every mutating external call needs an idempotency key.

(2) Store in database (durable). (3) Send to recipient via WebSocket. (4) If recipient is offline, they'll see it when they fetch chat history on reconnect. title: Bug-Finding Challenge: CDN with synchronized TTL expiry causing thundering herd
description: Bug-finding challenge prompt: 'Your CDN caches product pages with a 1-hour TTL. At peak traffic (100K requests/sec), you notice your origin servers get crushed exactly every hour. What is the bug?' Expected answer: The bug is SYNCHRONIZED CACHE EXPIRY. All product pages were cached at approximately the same time (e.g., after a deployment or cache flush), so they all expire at the same time. At the expiry moment, 100K requests simultaneously hit the origin servers (thundering herd), potentially crashing them. Fixes: (1) Add jitter to TTL: instead of exactly 3600s, use 3600 + random(0, 600) so expirations are spread over 10 minutes. (2) Stale-while-revalidate: serve stale content while asynchronously refreshing in the background. (3) Request coalescing (also called 'single-flight'): if 1000 requests arrive for the same expired key simultaneously, only ONE request goes to origin; the rest wait for that result. (4) Proactive cache warming before expiry. Learning objective: Cache stampede/thundering herd is one of the most common production issues with caching. TTL jitter and request coalescing are essential patterns.

The retry re-sends the same notification, and without dedup, the user gets it again. Each subsequent timeout+retry adds another duplicate. (2) Regional API deployments — deploy app servers in US, EU, Asia, and Australia regions behind a global load balancer with geo-routing (Route 53 latency-based routing). (3) Regional read replicas — each region has a database read replica for fast local reads. (4) Writes to nearest primary with async replication, OR use a globally distributed database (CockroachDB, Spanner) for strong consistency. (5) Edge caching for API responses where possible. Key tradeoff: Multi-region writes with strong consistency (Spanner) adds ~50ms for consensus. If you need consistent writes under 100ms globally, you need to accept eventual consistency or use CRDTs. Learning objective: Sub-100ms global latency requires data locality — you can't beat the speed of light, so bring the data closer to the user.

title: Scenario Selection: Startup with $5K/month budget needs real-time chat for 100K users
description: Scenario-based selection prompt: 'You are a startup with a $5K/month infrastructure budget. You need to build real-time chat supporting 100K monthly active users with message persistence, read receipts, and typing indicators. Design the most cost-effective architecture.' Expected answer: Cost-optimized stack: (1) WebSocket server: 2x c5.xlarge EC2 instances ($125/mo each) behind an ALB ($16/mo). Each handles ~50K concurrent WebSocket connections. (2) Database: Single RDS PostgreSQL db.t3.medium ($35/mo) for message persistence and user data — at 100K MAU, a single well-indexed Postgres handles the load easily. (3) Redis: Single ElastiCache t3.small ($25/mo) for presence (online/offline), typing indicators, and recent message caching. (4) S3 for media attachments ($5/mo). (5) Total: ~$350/mo, well within budget. What NOT to do: Don't use Kafka (overkill at this scale), don't use microservices (3-5 engineers can't support them), don't use DynamoDB (per-request pricing gets expensive with chat's high read/write volume). Scale-up path: When hitting limits, add WebSocket servers horizontally, add Redis Pub/Sub for cross-server messaging, and add Postgres read replicas. Learning objective: Good system design at startup scale means choosing boring technology that's cost-effective, not over-engineering with distributed systems you don't need yet.
